context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on Problem Set 8 for vibrations and waves, and I’m pretty stuck on 8.1. I think it’s about finding normal modes of the 3×3 bead grid, but I’m not sure how to set up the equations of motion correctly.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Before writing any equations, think about a single bead in the interior of the grid. What forces act on it due to the strings connected to it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting on 8.1 and needs to set up equations of motion. Rather than scaffolding the full setup, I should ask a targeted Socratic question to get them thinking about what forces act on a single bead, which is the natural starting point. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think each bead gets restoring forces from its horizontal and vertical neighbors, since the motion is transverse in the \(z\)-direction. So for an interior bead, I’m guessing the force depends on differences like \(z_{j,k+1}-z_{j,k}\) and \(z_{j+1,k}-z_{j,k}\), but I’m not fully sure about the signs.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's the right physical picture. Now, think about just one interior bead at position (j,k). If you sum all four neighbor contributions (left, right, up, down) carefully, what does Newton's second law look like for that bead?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right physical intuition. They understand transverse motion and neighbor differences. I should affirm this and push them to write the full equation of motion for a single interior bead, rather than spoon-feeding the signs. A targeted question will move them forward.

exchanges.student:
I think it should be something like

\[
m \ddot z_{j,k}=T_h(z_{j,k+1}-z_{j,k})+T_h(z_{j,k-1}-z_{j,k})+T_v(z_{j+1,k}-z_{j,k})+T_v(z_{j-1,k}-z_{j,k}).
\]

So maybe after collecting terms,

\[
m \ddot z_{j,k}=T_h(z_{j,k+1}+z_{j,k-1}-2z_{j,k})+T_v(z_{j+1,k}+z_{j-1,k}-2z_{j,k}).
\]

I’m assuming that’s only for interior beads, and edge beads would use the fixed boundary as zero displacement?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on both counts. Now, given the separable structure of that equation — horizontal and vertical terms are independent — what form would you try for a normal mode solution z_{j,k}(t)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the equation of motion for interior beads and correctly identified that boundary conditions set displacements to zero at the edges. This is a key milestone. Now I should push them toward the normal mode ansatz — how do they guess a solution form that separates variables? This is the crucial next step.

exchanges.student:
I’d try a product form, maybe

\[
z_{j,k}(t)=A_{j,k}\cos(\omega t)
\]

first, and then more specifically

\[
A_{j,k}=X_k Y_j
\]

so

\[
z_{j,k}(t)=X_k Y_j \cos(\omega t).
\]

I’m not totally sure if I should use sines right away because of the fixed boundaries, but I think that’s probably where it’s going.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. Now substitute that ansatz into your equation of motion. What does the equation reduce to after dividing through by X_k Y_j cos(ωt)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right ansatz. Now I should push them to substitute this into the equation of motion and see what it reduces to — this will reveal the separation into two independent 1D eigenvalue problems, which is the key insight for finding normal modes.

exchanges.student:
I think it becomes

\[
-m\omega^2 X_kY_j
=
T_h(Y_j)(X_{k+1}+X_{k-1}-2X_k)
+
T_v(X_k)(Y_{j+1}+Y_{j-1}-2Y_j).
\]

Then dividing by \(X_kY_j\),

\[
-m\omega^2
=
T_h\frac{X_{k+1}+X_{k-1}-2X_k}{X_k}
+
T_v\frac{Y_{j+1}+Y_{j-1}-2Y_j}{Y_j}.
\]

So since one term only depends on \(k\) and the other only on \(j\), I think each part has to be a constant. Is that the right separation step?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. You now have two independent 1D problems. You've likely seen the 1D beaded string before — what are the solutions X_k and Y_j that satisfy fixed boundary conditions at the ends?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly performed the separation of variables and identified that each term must be a constant. This is exactly right. Now I should push them to solve each 1D problem — they should recognize these as the familiar 1D beaded string equations with fixed boundary conditions, whose solutions they likely know.

exchanges.student:
I think for fixed ends the discrete modes are sine-shaped, so maybe

\[
X_k=\sin\!\left(\frac{p\pi k}{4}\right), \qquad
Y_j=\sin\!\left(\frac{q\pi j}{4}\right)
\]

with \(p,q=1,2,3\), since there are 3 beads and then the boundaries are at 0 and 4 in the index sense.

I’m a little unsure why it’s \(4\) in the denominator, but I think it’s because for 3 interior masses the fixed endpoints add two more positions, so \(N+1=4\).
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning about N+1=4 is exactly right. Now substitute your sine solutions back into the separated equation to find ω² as a function of p and q.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the sine solutions and correctly reasoned about why N+1=4. This is solid work. Now I should push them to complete the problem by finding the frequencies — they should substitute X_k and Y_j back into the separated equation to get ω² in terms of p and q.

exchanges.student:
I think the 1D discrete eigenvalue gives

\[
X_{k+1}+X_{k-1}-2X_k
=
-4\sin^2\!\left(\frac{p\pi}{8}\right)X_k,
\]

and similarly

\[
Y_{j+1}+Y_{j-1}-2Y_j
=
-4\sin^2\!\left(\frac{q\pi}{8}\right)Y_j.
\]

So then

\[
m\omega_{pq}^2
=
4T_h\sin^2\!\left(\frac{p\pi}{8}\right)
+
4T_v\sin^2\!\left(\frac{q\pi}{8}\right),
\]

or

\[
\omega_{pq}^2
=
\frac{4}{m}\left[
T_h\sin^2\!\left(\frac{p\pi}{8}\right)
+
T_v\sin^2\!\left(\frac{q\pi}{8}\right)
\right].
\]

I think that gives the 9 modes for \(p,q=1,2,3\).
exchanges.tutor: That's correct — well done on part (a). Now for part (b), with Tv = 1000·Th, the two terms in ω² are very different in scale. How does that help you think about ordering the 9 modes by increasing frequency?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully derived the normal mode frequencies. This is excellent work completing part (a). Now I should direct them to part (b), where they need to rank the 9 modes by frequency using Tv = 1000*Th and draw the sign diagrams. I should prompt them to think about how to order the modes without doing it for them.

exchanges.student: I think the \(T_v\) term will dominate a lot, so the ordering should be determined mostly by \(q\) first, not \(p\). So all the modes with \(q=1\) should come first, then \(q=2\), then \(q=3\), and within each group I’d order by \(p=1,2,3\).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now for the sign diagrams: the sign of bead (j,k) is determined by the sign of sin(pπk/4)·sin(qπj/4). Try working out the signs for the first mode (p=1, q=1) — what do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the ordering logic. Now I should push them to actually draw the sign diagrams for each mode, which requires them to think about what the sine functions look like at j=1,2,3 and k=1,2,3. I should prompt them to work through at least the first mode and the logic for signs.

exchanges.student:
For \(p=1\), \(\sin(\pi k/4)\) is positive for \(k=1,2,3\).  
For \(q=1\), \(\sin(\pi j/4)\) is also positive for \(j=1,2,3\).

So every product is positive, which gives

\[
\begin{matrix}
+ & + & +\\
+ & + & +\\
+ & + & +
\end{matrix}
\]

That matches the example, so I think I’m on the right track.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect, that matches. You have everything you need now — go ahead and work out the remaining 8 modes the same way. One thing to be careful about: make sure you're consistent about which index (j or k) corresponds to rows vs. columns in your diagram.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has confirmed the first mode correctly. Rather than walking through all 9 modes together, I should prompt them to work through the remaining 8 on their own, since they now clearly have the method. I can remind them to be careful about the k and j index ordering in their diagrams.

exchanges.student: Okay, thanks — I think I can do that now. I might come back to check the \(p=2\) or \(q=2\) sign patterns, because those zeros in the middle are where I get mixed up.
exchanges.tutor: Good. When you're unsure about zeros, just ask yourself: for which values of k or j does the sine function equal zero exactly? That's all there is to it. Come back if you get stuck, and when you're done with 8.1 we can move on to 8.2.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling confident and ready to work independently. I should briefly encourage them and give a small hint about where zeros come from without giving anything away, then suggest they move on to problem 8.2 if they finish.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session on Problem 8.1. Every key reasoning step was elicited from the student rather than given outright: the equation of motion, the separability argument, the ansatz, the substitution, the 1D eigenvalue, and the sign-diagram logic were all drawn out through targeted questions. The tutor built on each correct student response, gave concise bite-sized prompts, maintained a warm and encouraging tone, and provided genuine process-level guidance (e.g., the hint about checking where sine equals zero). No submission-ready content was produced by the tutor; the student did the intellectual work.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never stated a conclusion before the student reasoned toward it, never rewrote student work, and never carried the reasoning itself. Each turn posed a focused question that advanced the student one step. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student progress (e.g., 'Exactly right on both counts', 'Good instinct') and built directly on it; no restart from scratch occurred. No diagnostic failures were observed. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level guidance throughout, including the concrete hint about zeros and the reminder about index consistency. 2.1: The conversation progressed steadily without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The student stayed on task; no misunderstanding or drift required correction. 3.1: Responses were short, focused, and interactive — no monologues. 3.2: Tone was supportive and formative throughout; no grading language was used.
